text: The robotic sigmoid resection begins with the surgeon separating the small bowel from the colon, addressing adhesions in the area of disease. The robotic arms are docked, and the surgeon works to restore normal anatomy, dealing with unusual attachments between the terminal ileum mesentery and the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon carefully manipulates a loop of ileum fused to the medial aspect of the proximal sigmoid, searching for natural tissue planes to avoid injury to the bowel or its mesentery.

The surgeon carefully separates the loop of small bowel from the medial aspect of the sigmoid mesentery, revealing congenital attachments between the TI mesentery and the sigmoid colon. The absence of ureteral catheters is noted, which could have aided in the dissection process.

The surgeon attempts to access the area behind the mesosigmoid, aiming for the sacral promontory, which is considered a zone of safety. The sigmoid colon is visibly adhered to the right pelvic side wall, appearing as congenital attachments rather than resulting from diverticular disease. The right ureter is identified, and the surgeon carefully maneuvers around the bowel, which is planned for resection.

The surgeon carefully navigates into the true pelvis, aiming to separate the sigmoid colon from the pelvic sidewall while identifying the right ureter, which is visible over the psoas muscle. The dissection proceeds in the mesorectal plane, posterior to the rectal mesentery, with the ureter observed along the right aspect of the rectum, positioned more medially than usual. The surgeon maintains dissection within the retrorectal space, known as the 'holy plane,' similar to a total mesorectal excision (TME) for rectal cancer.

The surgeon has freed the sigmoid colon into the pelvis and is examining a thickened fold of mesentery near the root of the superior hemorrhoidal artery and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The left common iliac artery is visible, and the surgeon is searching for the left ureter while carefully staying within the known anatomical plane.

The surgeon lifts the mesentery off the retroperitoneum to minimize the risk of injury to the ureter or gonadal vessels. The mesentery is unfolded, and the surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection before switching to lateral dissection. The proximal resection margin is marked to maintain orientation during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and demonstrates the mobilization of the splenic flexure, noting movement along the gonadal vessels that can resemble a ureter. The procedure is performed using four 8mm ports positioned from the left upper to right lower abdomen, with the patient in steep Trendelenburg and left side up. The lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure is shown, with the spleen visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the pancreas and begins mobilizing the mesentery of the splenic flexure away from the pancreas. The pancreas becomes visible as the surgeon carefully lifts the mesentery, ensuring not to inadvertently move behind the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a pre-created window above the superior hemorrhoidal vessels to take the mesentery to the desired proximal margin. The distal margin is also identified. The surgeon prepares for a transrectal extraction of the specimen, using an energy device to carefully dissect through the mesentery, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal vessels.

The surgeon cleans the upper rectum and checks perfusion before selecting the proximal and distal margins for resection. Under white light, the surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to divide the tissue at the proximal margin, followed by division at the distal margin.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum lower to facilitate transrectal extraction, revealing dissected planes for better manipulation. The specimen is chopped into smaller pieces for extraction using a ring clamp, with the surgeon individually extracting each piece from below without an assistant.

The surgeon performs a sewn end-to-end anastomosis using 8mm trocars, avoiding linear and circular staplers. The back row is completed with 2-0 PDS sutures, followed by an inner layer with 3-0 V-Loc sutures. The suture is shown coming out from the other side, inverting the common anastomotic opening.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis by bringing the two sutures to meet in the middle, finishing the second outer anterior row of 2-0 PDS sutures. The anastomosis is constructed with two layers of absorbable sutures, ensuring a wide open lumen without narrowing.
The surgeon ties the final suture of the anastomosis and performs a leak test, confirming there is no tension or leakage in the bowel connection.
